title: A real site, from scratch
checkLabels.t14: The images are in the listings
checkLabels.t16: The right thing is on the side
why.empty: The editor is empty. You deleted the whole site.
why.untouched: This is exactly the code I gave you. Nothing has been changed.
hint2.untouched: Nothing gets deleted or added. You wrap — each thing in the tag that says what it is.
why.not-closed: A tag is open and never closed.
hint2.not-closed: Wrappers nest inside each other, they do not overlap. Close a <header> after opening a <nav> and the order is wrong.
why.lost: You deleted the content. The task was to wrap the page in meaning, not remove it. Bring back the title, the images and the prices.
why.no-header: The site title stands bare. It is the top part — say so.
hint2.no-header: There are two things at the top: the shop name and the menu. They belong together.
hint3.no-header:
A similar case:

Bakery

...
. One wrapper holds both.
hint4.no-header: Open <header> above the <h1> and close it after the menu.
why.no-nav: The menu is still a paragraph with bars. Remember lesson 35? A list of links inside <nav>.
hint2.no-nav: The paragraph Home | Bikes | Contact is not a menu. It is a sentence in which you drew a menu with bars.
hint4.no-nav: Wrap the menu in <nav>. Then work inside it.
why.no-list: The menu is not a list. Home, Bikes and Contact are three things of one kind — that is a <ul> with three <li>. Bars do not make a list.
hint2.no-list: A reader counts the items and says: list of 3 items. A paragraph with bars is not counted — it is one long line.
hint4.no-list: Inside the <nav> put a <ul>. Each of the three words becomes an <li>.
why.no-links: The menu items are not links. A menu that leads nowhere is a list of words. You know <a href> from lesson 33.
hint2.no-links: Each item should lead to a page. Three items, three links.
hint3.no-links:
A similar case:
  • Contact
    • . The link goes INSIDE the item.
hint4.no-links: The files do not exist yet and that is fine. Write index.html, bikes.html, contact.html.
why.still-bars: The bars are still there. You drew a menu with | and it is still in the text — you now have a real menu, so the drawing is redundant.
hint2.still-bars: Search the editor with Ctrl+F for the | character. Separating the items is the list's job, not a bar's.
why.no-main: There is no main content. What is the point of this page — the bikes. The menu and the footer repeat everywhere.
hint2.no-main: The person came for the bikes. Everything else is setting.
hint4.no-main: Wrap the two listings in <main>. The menu, the side note and the bottom line stay outside it.
why.no-article: The listings are not standalone things. Each bike is a heading, an image and a price — lift it out of the page and it still makes sense. That is an <article>. One for each of the two.
hint2.no-article: Two bikes means two listings. Each listing is three lines: h2, img, p.
hint3.no-article:
A similar case:

Bread

Price: 2 leva

. The second loaf is a second article.
hint4.no-article: Open <article> before the first <h2> and close it after the first price. Then the same for the second bike. Both articles live inside <main>.
why.img-outside: An image has been left outside its listing. The heading, the image and the price are one thing — all three go inside one <article>.
hint2.img-outside: Check where you close the </article>. Close it after the heading and the image stays outside.
why.no-aside: Similar shops nearby is not part of the main thing. It is something on the side — <aside>.
hint2.no-aside: The person came to buy a bike from THIS shop. Other shops are a side note, not the point.
hint4.no-aside: Wrap that paragraph in <aside>. It sits outside <main>.
why.aside-empty: There is something on the side, but the wrong thing is in it. The side note is the paragraph about similar shops.
hint2.aside-empty: Check what you put inside the <aside>. The listings are the main thing — they are not on the side.
why.no-footer: The copyright is the bottom part of the site.
hint2.no-footer: The bottom line with 2026 is a formality. There is a tag that says exactly that: the content ends here.
why.many-main: There is more than one <main>. It is exactly one per page. Two main contents means zero.
hint2.many-main: The listings are one main thing, not two. Both articles live inside a single <main>.
blocks.0.text: Here is a site that works. Headings, images, everything shows. Not a single <div> — it is better than most beginner sites.
blocks.1.text: And yet it has no structure at all. Everything is stacked one under the other, like a shopping list.
blocks.3.text: Go through the page and name each thing out loud: this is the site title. This is the menu. These are the two bikes — they are the point. This on the side is not important. This at the bottom is the formalities.
blocks.4.text: You see it instantly. The browser does not.
blocks.5.text: To it everything is equal: heading, paragraph, image, paragraph. No difference between the menu and the merchandise.
blocks.7.text: I am not giving you the code. You know every tag from the last lesson.
blocks.8.text: A few questions to ask yourself: which is the top part? Where does it end? Which is the main thing — the thing the person came for?
blocks.9.text: Is each bike standalone? If you lift it out of the page and put it elsewhere — does it still make sense?
blocks.10.text: Is similar shops nearby part of the main thing, or something on the side?
blocks.12.text: Look at it: Home | Bikes | Contact — one paragraph with bars.
blocks.13.text: Remember lesson 35? Three things of one kind are a list. Each item is a link. And a group of links that moves you around the site is navigation.
blocks.14.text: Do not leave it that way just because it works. And once you fix it — remove the bars. They were a drawing of a list. Now you have a list.
blocks.16.text: When you are done, read your code aloud — only the tags.
blocks.17.text: Header. Nav. List of three links. Main. Article. Heading. Image. Paragraph. Article. Heading…
blocks.18.text: If that sounds like a description of a shop — you did it.
blocks.19.text: If it sounds like heading, paragraph, image, paragraph, paragraph — you have not said anything yet.
blocks.21.text: Notice the preview will not change at all. Not a single pixel.
blocks.22.text: Getting used to this yet? The most important work in HTML is invisible.
blocks.23.text: Tomorrow, when you add CSS, everything will turn beautiful in ten minutes. But the meaning you put in today cannot be bolted on later.
blocks.24.text: Beauty is skin deep. Structure is not.
